summary:
El Argar was a significant Bronze Age culture of southeastern Iberia, centered in the provinces of Almería and Murcia in modern Spain, flourishing approximately 1800–1300 BC. First identified at the type site of El Argar near Antas in Almería, it represents one of the earliest and most complex Bronze Age societies in the Iberian Peninsula. El Argar communities inhabited hilltop settlements with defensive walls, organized in ways that suggest hierarchical social structures with distinct elite and non-elite groups.

El Argar is distinguished by a highly standardized burial practice in which the dead were interred individually in ceramic jars or stone cists placed beneath the floors of houses, with grave goods reflecting the social status of the deceased. Analysis of burial assemblages has identified clear social stratification, with some individuals buried with elaborate bronze weapons, silver ornaments, and ceramic vessels, while others were interred with minimal or no goods. Evidence suggests that El Argar society may have been organized along early state-like lines, with centralized control over surplus agricultural production and craft manufacturing.

El Argar communities developed significant bronze metallurgy, and their sites have yielded large quantities of bronze objects including halberds, flat axes, and distinctive tanged daggers. Around 1300 BC the culture underwent a relatively sudden collapse, with settlements abandoned and the distinctive burial and material culture disappearing. This collapse has been linked to possible social unrest, ecological stress related to agricultural intensification, or disruptions to the long-distance exchange networks on which the culture depended.
